text: The lower esophageal sphincter or LES is a one-way valve that separates the esophagus from the stomach. When food or liquid is swallowed, the LES opens transiently to allow passage into the stomach. Otherwise, the sphincter remains closed to prevent stomach contents from flowing back up into the esophagus. However, for patients who suffer from reflux, the LES abnormally remains open allowing stomach secretions and contents to reflux up into the esophagus causing symptoms.

Should medical and lifestyle management fail to resolve reflux symptoms, such patients may be a candidate for surgical intervention which involves physically tightening the LES. The Nissen fundoplication or stomach wrap is the standard approach to surgically tighten the LES as shown here.

The surgeon performs a Nissen fundoplication by wrapping the fundus of the stomach around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) using laparoscopic instruments inserted through small abdominal incisions.

The esophagus and stomach are visible, having been freed for surgical manipulation. A rubber band is looped around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), with the esophagus positioned above and the stomach below. A white mesh is seen at the top, indicating the site of a repaired hiatal hernia.
The surgeon pushes the top part of the stomach behind and around the esophagus, moving it back and forth to ensure the wrap is loose enough to maintain adequate blood flow and prevent tissue damage.
The surgeon places the first suture, securing the stomach wrap by stitching stomach tissue to stomach tissue.
The surgeon places the second suture through the esophagus, followed by the final third suture through the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), securing the stomach wrap in place.
The completion of the Nissen fundoplication is visually confirmed, with the top part of the stomach wrapped around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), resembling a belt-like structure.
